title: GraphQL Intro
content:
GraphQL Introduction

What is GraphQL?

GraphQL is a query language for APIs and a runtime for fulfilling those queries with your existing data. It provides a complete and understandable description of the data in your API, gives clients the power to ask for exactly what they need and nothing more.

  • Declarative: Clients specify exactly what data they need.
  • Hierarchical: The query matches the shape of the data returned.
  • Strongly Typed: The schema defines what's possible, ensuring data integrity.
  • Single Endpoint: Usually works through a single HTTP endpoint, unlike REST.
  • No Over-fetching: Get all the data you need in a single request.

title: Schema & Types
content:
Schema & Types

Defining Your Data

The Schema is the core of any GraphQL server implementation. Use the Schema Definition Language (SDL) to define your types.

  • Scalar Types: Int, Float, String, Boolean, ID.
  • Object Types: Represent a kind of object you can fetch.
  • Query Type: The entry point for Read operations.
  • Mutation Type: The entry point for Write operations.
GraphQL Schema Example
type User {
  id: ID!
  username: String!
  email: String
}

type Query {
  me: User
  user(id: ID!): User
}

title: Queries
content:
GraphQL Queries

Fetching Data

Queries allow the client to request specific fields on objects. This prevents over-fetching data.

Simple Query
query GetUserProfile {
  user(id: "123") {
    username
    email
  }
}

In the response, the server will return a JSON object with the exact same structure as the query.

title: Mutations
content:
Mutations

Modifying Data

Mutations are used to create, update, or delete data on the server. Like queries, mutations can also return the modified object.

Mutation Example
mutation CreateUser($name: String!) {
  createUser(name: $name) {
    id
    name
    createdAt
  }
}

title: Variables
content:
Variables

Dynamic Queries

Variables allow you to reuse queries with different values, which is safer and cleaner than using string interpolation.

Variable Usage
query GetUser($userId: ID!) {
  user(id: $userId) {
    name
  }
}

Variables are passed as a separate JSON object in the request.

title: Directives
content:
Directives

Conditional Logic

Directives let you dynamically change the structure and shape of your queries using variables.

  • @include(if: Boolean): Only include this field if the argument is true.
  • @skip(if: Boolean): Skip this field if the argument is true.
Directive Example
query GetHero($heroId: ID!, $withDetails: Boolean!) {
  hero(id: $heroId) {
    name
    friends @include(if: $withDetails) {
      name
    }
  }
}

title: Resolvers
content:
Resolvers

Fetching the Data

A resolver is a function that populates the data for a single field in your schema. It can fetch data from any source (DB, REST API, etc.).

Resolver Example (JavaScript)
const resolvers = {
  Query: {
    user: (parent, args, context, info) => {
      return database.users.findById(args.id);
    },
  },
};

title: Authentication
content:
Authentication

Securing Your Data

Authentication in GraphQL is usually handled at the context level. Resolvers then check the user's identity before returning data.

  • HTTP Headers: Pass tokens (like JWT) in the Authorization header.
  • Global Guards: Check authentication before a query even reaches the resolvers.
  • Field Level: Restrict specific fields to specific user roles.

title: Error Handling
content:
Error Handling

Managing Issues

GraphQL returns an errors array in the response if something goes wrong. A query can also return partial data along with errors.

Error Response Example
{
  "data": {
    "user": null
  },
  "errors": [
    {
      "message": "User not found",
      "path": ["user"],
      "extensions": {
        "code": "NOT_FOUND"
      }
    }
  ]
}

title: Introspection
content:
Introspection

Discovering the Schema

GraphQL allows you to query its own schema to discover what types and fields are available. This is used by tools like GraphiQL and Apollo Studio.

Introspection Query
query GetSchema {
  __schema {
    types {
      name
      kind
    }
  }
}

title: Apollo & Relay
content:
GraphQL Clients

Frontend Integration

Specialized libraries help manage GraphQL state and queries on the frontend.

  • Apollo Client: The most popular, feature-rich library with caching and local state management.
  • Relay: A production-ready client by Meta, designed for high performance and scalability.
  • urql: A lightweight and highly customizable alternative.

title: Pagination
content:
Pagination

Handling Large Lists

When fetching lists of data, pagination is essential to keep responses small and fast.

  • Offset-based: Simple but less efficient for deep pages (e.g., skip 100, take 10).
  • Cursor-based: More reliable for real-time data and large datasets.
Cursor Pagination Query
query GetUsers($after: String) {
  users(first: 10, after: $after) {
    pageInfo {
      hasNextPage
      endCursor
    }
    edges {
      node {
        id
        name
      }
    }
  }
}

title: Filtering & Sorting
content:
Filtering & Sorting

Refining Queries

GraphQL doesn't prescribe a specific filtering syntax, but it's common to pass a filter object as an argument.

Filter & Sort Query
query GetProducts($filter: ProductFilter, $sort: ProductSort) {
  products(filter: $filter, sort: $sort) {
    name
    price
  }
}

title: Fragments
content:
Fragments

Reusable Field Sets

Fragments let you define a set of fields that can be reused across multiple queries.

Fragment Example
fragment UserFields on User {
  id
  username
  avatarUrl
}

query GetUsers {
  users {
    ...UserFields
  }
}

title: Input Types
content:
Input Types

Complex Arguments

Instead of passing many separate arguments to a mutation, you can define an input type to group them.

Input Type Definition
input CreateUserInput {
  name: String!
  email: String!
  bio: String
}

mutation CreateUser($input: CreateUserInput!) {
  createUser(input: $input) {
    id
  }
}

title: Enums & Custom Scalars
content:
Enums & Custom Scalars

Extending the Type System

Enums restrict a field to a specific set of values. Custom scalars allow you to define types like Date or Email.

Enum Example
enum UserRole {
  ADMIN
  USER
  GUEST
}

type User {
  role: UserRole!
}

title: Subscriptions
content:
Subscriptions

Real-time Data

Subscriptions allow clients to listen for real-time updates from the server, typically over WebSockets.

Subscription Example
subscription OnMessageAdded($chatId: ID!) {
  messageAdded(chatId: $chatId) {
    id
    text
    author
  }
}

title: Performance & DataLoader
content:
Performance

Solving the N+1 Problem

A common issue in GraphQL is the N+1 problem, where a query triggers many separate database calls. DataLoader is a utility to batch and cache these requests.

DataLoader Concept
// Batch 10 users in one query instead of 10 separate queries
const userLoader = new DataLoader(keys => myBatchGetUsers(keys));

title: Best Practices
content:
Best Practices

Scaling GraphQL

  • Naming: Use clear, descriptive names for types and fields.
  • Evolution: Add new fields instead of versioning the API.
  • Security: Limit query depth and complexity to prevent DoS attacks.
  • Documentation: Use descriptions in your schema for automatic docs.

title: Course Summary
content:
GraphQL Mastery 🏆

You're a GraphQL Expert!

You have completed the full 20-lesson GraphQL course! From basic queries to advanced performance optimization, you now have the tools to build world-class APIs.

  • Fundamentals: Schemas, Queries, Mutations, and Resolvers.
  • Advanced: Directives, Introspection, and Subscriptions.
  • Production: Performance, Security, and Best Practices.

Keep building and exploring the amazing world of GraphQL!
